teamObjective.explanation: With only two cards remaining and the opponents holding three each, the priority is to play out quickly. Leading the ace gives the highest chance of winning this trick so I can lead the 2 next and empty my hand.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has three cards but has made no plays, so their exact strength and suit distribution are unknown. They may be able to cover a single if the ace is beaten, but no reliable signal has been sent.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents each hold three cards and have not acted. Any of them could hold a trump 7, joker, or bomb. Because the hand is so short, they may try to seize control immediately rather than pass.

actionRationale.primaryReason: The ace of spades is the stronger of my two cards. Leading it now maximizes the chance of winning the trick uncontested and lets me play the remaining 2 on my next turn to finish my hand.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 2 is much more likely to be beaten immediately. If control passes to an opponent, I may never get a clean single lead for the ace, slowing my finish and relying on partner rescue.

risks: An opponent may play a trump 7, joker, or bomb over the ace and take control.
If the ace is beaten, my remaining 2 is very weak and may be hard to play on my own turn.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the ace first, I still keep the 2 in hand if I lose the trick, and if the ace wins I guarantee a next-turn finish. The alternative of leading the 2 gives up control from the start.

notes: Only the new_game event is public; all partner and opponent beliefs remain probabilistic.
